title: dataGenie — Multi-Agent Data Analytics Platform
tagline: Upload a CSV, ask in plain English, get back a chart, a written answer, and the SQL you can verify.
summary: Non-technical people shouldn't need to write SQL to explore their own data. dataGenie collapses the loop — ingest CSV / Excel / PDF / database, ask a question, get an interactive chart with a written explanation and confidence score, with the underlying query shown so you can verify.
resume_bullets: Designed a hybrid query router that classifies questions as simple, moderate, or complex and dispatches to direct DuckDB SQL or a ReAct loop with five specialist agents.
Built a Verification agent specifically to cross-check the other agents' outputs and catch hallucinations before they surface to the user.
Wrote a multi-provider LLM layer with per-model token counting and a custom benchmark harness comparing cost, latency, and quality across Claude, OpenAI, and Ollama.
demonstrates: Hybrid routing as a real product decision, not a default — knowing when to spend tokens and when not to.
In-process analytics stack (DuckDB columnar SQL + Parquet on disk + pandas / scipy / statsmodels) — fast and self-contained.
Multi-agent orchestration with a dedicated Verification step — catching hallucinations is part of the system, not an afterthought.
proof: 10+ Plotly chart types; PNG / SVG / CSV / Excel / PDF export.
1,193-line conversation engine tracking current dataset, columns, and prior results for follow-up queries.
outcomes: Runs locally via `docker compose up`; full stack (FastAPI + Next.js 16 + DuckDB + Redis / Celery) wired and tested end-to-end.
Next: hosted demo, real-world data testing on >1GB datasets, token-budget enforcement.
constraints: Single 154-file monolithic commit representing ~4 weeks of work — feature-complete MVP, zero production hours.
Multi-provider fallback only earns its keep because the simple path stays cheap; the router has to be honest about complexity.

case_study_sections.title: What I built
case_study_sections.summary: A complexity router classifies queries as simple, moderate, or complex and dispatches either to direct DuckDB SQL or to a ReAct loop. Five specialist agents — Data Quality, Statistical, Visualization, Explanation, Verification — coordinated by an orchestrator. The Verification agent specifically exists to cross-check the others and catch hallucinations. A multi-provider LLM layer (Claude → OpenAI → Ollama) with per-model token counting and a benchmark harness comparing cost, latency, and quality across providers. An in-process analytics stack: DuckDB columnar SQL + Parquet on disk + SQLite metadata; pandas / scipy / statsmodels for stats; 10+ Plotly chart types; PNG / SVG / CSV / Excel / PDF export. A 1,193-line conversation engine that tracks the current dataset, columns mentioned, and previous results so follow-ups like 'compare those regions' actually work.

case_study_sections.title: One sharp decision
case_study_sections.summary: Hybrid query routing instead of pure-SQL or pure-agent. The hardest design call on the project. A single path is wrong both ways — pure SQL can't handle 'compare Q3 vs Q4 trends, break down by region, explain what changed,' and a ReAct loop for 'how many rows?' is absurd. The split lets the simple path stay sub-second and the agentic path spend tokens only where they earn ROI. That same split is what makes the multi-provider fallback meaningful — cheap providers handle the easy 80%, expensive ones get reserved for the work that needs them.

case_study_sections.title: One real struggle
case_study_sections.summary: Scope. dataGenie shipped as a single 154-file monolithic commit with 448 tests passing on my laptop and zero production hours. Feature-complete MVP, no real users, no >1GB dataset stress-tested, no token budget enforcement. The recovery — already visible in how I'm now shipping duSraBheja — is the habit of shipping smaller, deploying earlier, and letting real usage drive the next round of agents rather than designing them up front.

case_study_sections.title: Outcome
case_study_sections.summary: Runs locally via `docker compose up`. Full stack wired and tested. The next milestones are real-world data testing, performance work for >1GB datasets, and a hosted demo so the platform stops being a private repo and starts being a thing other people can poke.
